questions: What exactly causes the tar extraction to fail on Windows with Git for Windows installed?
Where is the bug located in the codebase and what is the exact failing code?
Are there other execSync/child_process tar invocations in the update module beyond the known one?
What JavaScript tar library best fits this project's constraints (ESM, Node >=18, no native deps, zero current runtime deps)?
What is the exact tarball format the extraction must handle?
What post-extraction contract must the replacement satisfy?
Could the bug be fixed by quoting paths differently or using --force-local instead of replacing the binary?

answers.answer: GNU tar (shipped with Git for Windows via MSYS2/mingw) interprets colon characters in path arguments as remote host designators using traditional Unix remote-tape syntax (host:path). When the staging path contains a Windows drive letter like C:\Users\...\staging\abc123.tgz, GNU tar parses 'C' as a hostname and attempts a network connection, producing: 'tar (child): Cannot connect to C: resolve failed'. This is intrinsic to GNU tar's command-line parser and not a shell escaping issue. Windows' built-in bsdtar (C:\Windows\System32\tar.exe) does not have this behavior, but Git for Windows places its GNU tar earlier in PATH.
evidence: Ticket continuation context — exact error: 'tar (child): Cannot connect to C: resolve failed'
src/update/perform.ts:124 — execSync(`tar -xzf "${tarballPath}" -C "${stagingDir}"`, ...)
Staging paths include drive letters: perform.ts:16 — STAGING_BASE = join(homedir(), '.hlx', 'staging') which resolves to e.g. C:\Users\user\.hlx\staging on Windows
Reproduced from PowerShell (not Git Bash) — confirms this is a PATH ordering issue, not MSYS shell quirk

answers.answer: The bug is at src/update/perform.ts line 124, inside the performStagedUpdate function. The exact code is: execSync(`tar -xzf "${tarballPath}" -C "${stagingDir}"`, { stdio: 'pipe', timeout: 30_000 }). The error is caught at lines 128-131 and returns { success: false, error: 'Extraction failed: ...' }. The function's error handling contract is already correct (returns structured error, does not throw). Only the extraction mechanism itself needs replacement.
evidence: src/update/perform.ts:122-131 — extract block with execSync and catch
src/update/perform.ts:77-81 — function signature returning Promise<{ success: boolean; error?: string }>

answers.answer: There is exactly one tar invocation in the update module: perform.ts:124. The other execSync/child_process usages are: (1) perform.ts:33 — copyDirRecursive shells out to xcopy/cp-R for EXDEV fallback (not tar), (2) check.ts:30 — execSync('gh auth token') for GitHub token discovery (not tar), (3) validate.ts:38 — spawnSync('node', ...) for version check (not tar). Acceptance criteria #3 scopes removal to 'external tar invocation' specifically, so only perform.ts:124 needs replacement.
evidence: src/update/perform.ts:33 — execSync for xcopy/cp-R (EXDEV fallback)
src/update/check.ts:30 — execSync('gh auth token')
src/update/validate.ts:38 — spawnSync('node', [entrypoint, '--version'])

answers.answer: The best fit is the 'tar' package (isaacs/node-tar, npm package name 'tar'). It was rewritten in TypeScript for v7+, provides ESM/CJS hybrid exports, is pure JavaScript with no native build dependency, and is used by npm itself (extremely battle-tested). It offers a high-level tar.x({ file, cwd }) API that directly replaces the execSync call. It handles gzip decompression automatically for .tgz files. The alternative 'tar-stream' (mafintosh/tar-stream) is lower-level and would require manually writing directory creation, file writing, and permission handling code — significantly more implementation work for no practical benefit. The project currently has zero runtime dependencies, so adding 'tar' introduces a new dependency, but the ticket explicitly says adding a runtime dependency is in scope.
evidence: package.json — zero runtime dependencies currently, only devDependencies
Ticket continuation context — 'Adding a runtime dependency to package.json if needed for the JS tar library' is explicitly in scope
node-tar GitHub — rewritten in TypeScript, ESM/CJS hybrid via tshy, used by npm itself
Context7 tar-stream docs — lower-level API requires manual file writing per entry
Ticket requirements — 'no native build dependency', 'works without invoking external tar'

answers.answer: The tarball is a standard gzip-compressed tar (.tgz) with top-level entries (no nested prefix directory): dist/, skill-content/, package.json, build-metadata.json. It excludes *.test.js and *.test.d.ts. Created by GNU tar on ubuntu-latest in CI. This is a simple, standard format with no symlinks, long paths, or special attributes — any JS tar library will handle it correctly.
evidence: .github/workflows/build-release.yml:36-43 — tar -czf helix-cli.tgz --exclude='*.test.js' --exclude='*.test.d.ts' dist/ skill-content/ package.json build-metadata.json
scout/reference-map.json — confirms top-level entries with no nested prefix

answers.answer: After extraction, the staging directory must contain the files that validateStaged() checks: dist/index.js must exist, package.json must exist, and 'node dist/index.js --version' must produce output and exit 0. The swap step also expects dist/, skill-content/ (optional), package.json, and build-metadata.json (optional) at the staging directory root. The extraction must produce the same filesystem layout as the current tar command.
evidence: src/update/validate.ts:22 — existsSync(join(stagingDir, 'dist', 'index.js'))
src/update/validate.ts:30 — existsSync(join(stagingDir, 'package.json'))
src/update/validate.ts:38 — spawnSync('node', [entrypoint, '--version'])
src/update/perform.ts:160-163 — swap references stagedDist, stagedSkillContent, stagedPackageJson, stagedBuildMeta

answers.answer: No. Quoting paths differently does not help because the colon interpretation is intrinsic to GNU tar's argument parser, not a shell escaping issue. The --force-local flag exists in GNU tar to disable remote-host interpretation, but it is GNU-specific and not supported by macOS BSD tar, so using it would break macOS. Hardcoding C:\Windows\System32\tar.exe would still depend on an external binary (violating the ticket's goal) and assumes bsdtar's availability (only since Windows 10 1803). The ticket explicitly rejects these workarounds: 'Do not paper over the bug by detecting and rejecting GNU tar at runtime, or by hardcoding C:\Windows\System32\tar.exe. The fix must remove the shell dependency entirely.'
evidence: Ticket continuation context — explicit rejection of --force-local and System32 hardcoding
GNU tar man page — --force-local is GNU extension, not in POSIX tar or BSD tar
Ticket goal — 'no user-side toolchain required to install'

statement: The hlx update extraction step at src/update/perform.ts:124 shells out to system tar via execSync, which fails on Windows when GNU tar (from Git for Windows) is first in PATH because GNU tar interprets Windows drive-letter colons as remote-host syntax. The fix is to replace this single execSync('tar -xzf ...') call with an in-process extraction using the 'tar' npm package (isaacs/node-tar), which provides a direct tar.x({ file, cwd }) API, is pure JavaScript with no native dependencies, and is ESM-compatible. The change is confined to the extraction block in perform.ts (lines 122-131) plus adding 'tar' as a runtime dependency in package.json. A new test file must exercise extraction of a representative .tgz and verify the output layout. No other update module files, CI workflows, or validation logic need changes.
